Football.title: Why is Bournemouth v Manchester United on Friday? talkSPORT coverage of crucial Premier League clash
Football.content:
Why is Bournemouth v Manchester United on Friday? talkSPORT coverage of crucial Premier League clash

Bournemouth and Manchester United will both have qualification for European football in their sights when they meet tonight.

The 2025/26 Premier League season concludes on May 24 and there is still so much to play in the final weeks.

4 The Vitality Stadium hosts the opening match of the weekend Credit: Getty

Andoni Iraola has enjoyed another strong campaign at Bournemouth, who haven't lost in the league since January 3.

They are currently on a ten-match unbeaten run, although six have ended in a draw.

Bournemouth have also scored just the one goal across their last four matches following a goalless draw with Burnley on Saturday.

The Cherries are tenth in the table and seven points off sixth, which is the final spot for European qualification.

United on the other hand sit third and are right in the race for Champions League football.

Michael Carrick has been an excellent appointment since agreeing to take the reins until the end of the season in January.

He has won seven of his nine matches in charge, with the most recent being a 3-1 victory over Aston Villa.

Five points separate them and fifth in the Premier League, with a top four finish certainly an achievable outcome.

Why is Bournemouth v Manchester United on Friday night?

Bournemouth will host United at the Vitality Stadium tonight - Friday, March 20 - and kick-off is scheduled for 8pm.

The match will be played in this slot as part of a broadcast selection, with Sky Sports showing it on television.

4 Iraola has overseen an impressive run of form at Bournemouth Credit: Getty

talkSPORT will have live commentary of the Premier League match hosted by Hugh Woozencroft.

Sam Matterface will be alongside the former West Ham and Crystal Palace manager Alan Pardew.

Tune in to talkSPORT through the website via the live stream or find us on DAB digital radio through your smart speaker and on 1089 or 1053 AM.

Download and listen to all our live commentaries via the talkSPORT app.

Visit talkSPORT BET for all the latest odds and bet boosts for every big event.

Subscribe to our talkSPORT YouTube channel for news, views and analysis plus our regular goals show and for more, 

4 Carrick looks on course to guide United to the Champions League Credit: Getty

Bournemouth v Manchester United: Team news

Tyler Adams could miss out for Bournemouth after picking up an injury in training on Friday before their draw with Burnley.

Justin Kluivert remains out after undergoing surgery on his knee in January, and Lewis Cook is also unavailable with a hamstring injury.

Julio Soler has a similar issue, but Ben Gannon-Doak could make his first appearance since recovering from hamstring surgery.

Carrick looks set to be without Matthijs de Ligt, Patrick Dorgu and Lisandro Martinez once again on Friday.

However, he will have been pleased to see Mason Mount return to the bench when they hosted Villa at the weekend.

4 Bournemouth and United's last meeting ended in a dramatic 4-4 draw Credit: Getty

Bournemouth v Manchester United: What's been said?

United captain Bruno Fernandes picked up his 15th and 16th assists of the season against Villa.

The Premier League record during a single campaign is 20, with both Thierry Henry and Kevin De Bruyne hitting that total.

"Bruno has done it for a long period of time now," Carrick said.

"In the big moments, making a difference whether he's scoring or creating, he's normally around it. Maybe (he can break the record)...

"I don't think it's something you go chasing for. I think it happens naturally but if he does, then he's obviously created goals for us, so I'll be happy with that."

Football.title: Bayern Munich chief reveals astonishing sum they would pay for Harry Kane now
Football.content:
Bayern Munich chief reveals astonishing sum they would pay for Harry Kane now

Bayern Munich's president revealed that he would pay £150million (€173million) if he was to buy Harry Kane today.

In 2023, Kane became the most expensive signing in Bundesliga history when he joined the German giants in a transfer worth €110million (£100million).

3 Harry Kane has scored 50 goals in all competitions this season Credit: Getty

He joined the league champions on a four-year deal after leaving Tottenham as their all-time Premier League top scorer.



Reports revealed earlier this year that Bayern Munich are currently working on a deal to extend the England striker's contract beyond 2027.

Kane settled into Bundesliga life quickly and has scored 119 goals in 126 games, showing no signs of slowing down.

He is currently the leagues top goal scorer with 30 goals, 14 ahead of the second-highest goal threat Deniz Undav.

The ex-Tottenham player is already the league's most expensive player but the Bayern Munich president has said he would pay even more for him nowadays due to what he adds to the team.



Uli Hoeness said: "Today I’d buy him for €150m (£130m) because he’s a dream for Bayern Munich.

"A global figurehead, a good character, a role model for our youth, our 18-year-olds.



"He puts his arm around them and he tells them how they need to shoot the ball.”

Bayern are currently league leaders, sitting nine points above second place Borussia Dortmund.



However, the Bayern president has sent a warning to fellow German team Eintracht Frankfurt.

3 The ex-Tottenham striker has been called up for international duty during the March international break Credit: Getty

Their Bundesliga rivals have become the league's best sellers in recent years but are unable to be competitive from a sporting aspect.

Frankfurt have sold many of their players abroad for high fees in the last two years including Randal Kolo Mauni to Paris Saint-German for €95m (£82m) in 2023.

The striker remains out of Luis Enrique's plans for the French side, which has led to him being on loan at Tottenham for the last season.



Kolo Muani's time at the club has mirrored the Lilywhites disappointing campaign this season after only scoring four goals in 32 appearances.

He has been recently lined up to returning to Juventus after spending the second-half of last season in Italy where he scored ten goals in 22 matches.

Additionally, Omar Marmoush joined Manchester City for €75m (£64m)from Frankfurt, followed by Hugo Ekitike’s move to Liverpool in the summer for €95m (£82m).

3 Kane won his first career trophy by lifting the Bundesliga with Bayern Credit: Getty

Hoeness is not a fan of the clubs financial model and has advised them they should not sell their strongest players despite the money they're receiving.



He said: "Personally, I’m not a big fan of selling good players, I always say at FC Bayern we’re a club that buys players, not one that sells them.

"Axel Hellmann [Frankfurt's board spokesman] will come to realise that, in the long run, you lose substance with every sale.

"It’s nice to get €50m or €60m once in a while, but what are the consequences?"

Eintracht are having an underwhelming season after being knocked out early in the Champions League and the DFB-Pokal.

Meanwhile, Bayern are through to the Champions League quarter finals against Real Madrid and are nine points clear of winning the league with eight games left.



They have never received more than €45m (£38m) for a single player including when Robert Lewandowski went to Barcelona and Lucas Hernandez moved to PSG.

Football.title: Man United star’s World Cup hopes at risk as Michael Carrick issues concerning update
Football.content:
Manchester United interim boss Michael Carrick worryingly admitted that he has no timeline on the return of centre-back Matthijs de Ligt.

A back injury – initially described as a minor issue by former United head coach Ruben Amorim – has left the Dutch defender sidelined since the start of December.

4 Matthijs de Ligt was an ever present for Man United before suffering a back injury in early December Credit: Getty

After starting the Red Devils' first 13 games of the season, De Ligt’s season came to a grinding halt ahead of the match against West Ham on December 4.

The 26-year-old was expected to return for the clash with Wolves four days later however, four months on, the defender is yet to make his first-team comeback.

Asked whether the former Juventus and Bayern Munich centre-back would return to action before the end of the season, Carrick admitted he had no idea of when De Ligt would be fit enough to resume playing.

"It's difficult to say, just because it's taken that much time," Carrick said.

"With backs sometimes you think it's all right and all of a sudden it's not quite right.

"We're just being patient and working through it. We'll give him time and try to get him back as quickly as we can, but it's really difficult to say."

The news is also of concern to the Netherlands ahead of this summer’s World Cup.

De Ligt was selected by national team boss Ronald Koeman for the internationals in September and November.

But with the tournament a little under three months away and De Ligt missing out on the upcoming internationals, time is running out for him to prove his fitness.

Carrick was further quizzed on whether De Ligt may need to undergo surgery in a bid to correct the issue.

4 Michael Carrick says he has no idea when De Ligt will be ready to return Credit: Getty

4 De Ligt has never featured in a league match under Carrick Credit: Getty

"I don't want to get into that at this stage," he said.

"We'll try and do whatever we can to help and we're working through that now.

"I'm not trying to hide anything. He's improving. It's just not as quick as we all hope."

United are riding high after last weekend’s 3-1 win over Aston Villa which put them in the driving seat for Champions League qualification.

And they could further strengthen their grip on the top three with a win at Bournemouth on Friday evening – live on talkSPORT.

United hit three points above Aston Villa in the race for European qualification with Liverpool, Chelsea and Brentford also in the running.

4 Michael Carrick has quickly turned around Man United's fortunes with Champions League qualification looking likely

Carrick has eight games remaining to secure a top four finish and doing so would further enhance his chances of landing the head coach role on a permanent basis.

The former United midfielder has lost just one of his nine matches in charge with his team picking up 22 points from 27 available.

Whilst Carrick often displays a calm facade, he admitted he got angry with his players following the 2-1 defeat at Newcastle earlier this month – the only blemish on his record to date.

“Obviously we’re disappointed at times and so were disappointed with the result at Newcastle, so there’s feelings there,” he said.

“It’s a sport, elite performance. You’ve got to play with emotion and feeling and sometimes that is a bit more aggressive, it’s a bit more intense sometimes, so you’ve got to manage that.

“If I don’t manage that, you can’t expect the players to create the right emotion. So it’s certainly part of our role.”

News.title: Influencer who fled Dubai to avoid being hit by a missile is beaten up and robbed in beauty salon
News.content:
AN influencer who fled to Paris from Dubai to avoid being hit by Iranian missiles has been beaten up and mugged in a beauty salon.

The content creator was one of three women held up by an armed gang in Milia Esthétique – located in the wealthy 16th arrondissement of the French capital.





An investigator said “four masked individuals assaulted and threatened” the trio, Paris Match reported.

“The victims [including an influencer] were violently beaten, sprayed with tear gas, and threatened with a knife,” they said.

It is understood that the Russian influencer had been living in Dubai and was the owner of a cosmetic surgery clinic.

“During the robbery, she was taken to the basement and beaten again,” they added.

“One of the women also reported being stabbed.”

The women – who were attacked on Thursday evening – have not yet been identified.

They were still being treated for their injuries on Friday.



The thieves escaped with several items valued at an estimated £175,000 – including a solid gold necklace, a diamond ring, an iPhone and a Louis Vuitton suitcase.

Following a tip off, police spotted one of the alleged gang members sitting in a car later in the evening.

One of the victims had helped with their identification.

The luxury branded suitcase was found in the boot of the car.

After police conducted further surveillance, six men aged between 18 and 25 years old were arrested.



The Paris prosecutor’s office has launched an investigation into the armed robbery.

The source told Paris Match the investigation had been “entrusted to the 1st district of the Paris judicial police”.

In the days and weeks following the Iranian attacks on the United Arab Emirates, hundreds of high-profile influencers – including many Russian creators – have fled the region.

The missile and bomb assaults are retaliation for the Israeli and American attacks on Iran.



Dubai, once dubbed one of the safest places on earth, has been a haven for rich expats from all over the world.

Since the war began, emergency messages have urged residents and tourists to take shelter almost every night, as missile and drone attacks continue.



Crime in relation to influencers and high-end jewellery has become increasingly common in the City of Love.

Last year, seven men and a woman were found guilty of robbing Kim Kardashian at gunpoint in her hotel room in 2016, before making off with almost £8 million-worth of jewellery.

Kim had been in the city for Paris Fashion Week at the time of the chilling armed robbery.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“The crime was the most terrifying experience of my life, leaving a lasting impact on me and my family,” she revealed after the convictions.

Throughout the investigation, Kim said she regretted showing off her high-end jewellery and her location, in an attempt to impress her millions of fans.

News.title: Celeb ex ‘sent deepfake porn of wife to strangers who thought they were having affair’ in ‘digital Gisele Pelicot’ case
News.content:
WEB OF LIES Celeb ex ‘sent deepfake porn of wife to strangers who thought they were having affair’ in ‘digital Gisele Pelicot’ case

A CELEBRITY’S ex-husband allegedly sent deepfake porn of his wife to strangers who thought they were having an affair with her.

German TV presenter Collien Fernandes, 44, has accused her former partner of online sexual harassment in a case which has drawn comparison to Gisele Pelicot.





Christian Ulmen, 50, an actor and fellow presenter allegedly shared real and fake images of his then wife Fernandes with “hundreds of men”.

It started when Ulmen allegedly started creating various profiles with her name attached to appear as if they were her, Spiegel reported.

These accounts would then send nude pictures to other users, including AI-generated and real pornographic material, the outlet reported.

Those who received fake nude images of Fernandes were then under the impression that they were in contact or having an affair with the presenter.

Fernandes reportedly filed criminal charges in Berlin in November 2024 against unknown suspects for creating spoof accounts imitating her.

But in a horrifying twist, Fernandes now alleges that her own ex-husband is behind the accounts – and admitted to the claims himself.

She also filed charges against Ulmen in Spain late last year – with the report revealing that she had been the alleged victim of “identity theft via social networks” for nearly a decade.

Fernandes alleges her husband confessed to her on Christmas day 2024 in Hamburg.



He allegedly told her: “It was me, I did it.”

Speaking on the moment, Fernandes said: “It was like hearing the news of a death, I couldn’t talk, I couldn’t cry.”

She said her world collapsed after hearing the alleged confession.

The brave TV presenter said: “My body was stolen from me for years.”

Christian Ulmen has not made a public statement yet addressing the claims.



Spanish courts have reportedly “initiated so-called preliminary investigations”.

Ulmen is accused of using fake identities of his ex-wife for years.

Her report labels “hundreds” of men who were contacted via spoof accounts.



It also claims people had phone sex with her fake profiles at least 30 times.

Fernandes and Ulmen married in 2011, with the couple having a daughter born the following year.

The family moved to Spain in 2023 before making their separation public in 2025.

The allegations bear striking resemblance to Gisele Pelicot’s case – leading some to call the latest claims a digital version of the notorious crimes committed by Dominique Pelicot.



Also known as the Beast of Avignon, Dominique was found guilty of inviting dozens of men to rape Gisèle while she lay unconscious after being drugged.

Brave Gisèle, who waived her anonymity, became a symbol of resistance after revealing she had no memory of the assaults and had been abused by at least 72 men.

That landmark trial exposed a sprawling network of men recruited online, a vast archive of videos and photos documenting hundreds of assaults.

It also revealed a sick grooming pattern in which Pelicot’s husband mixed powerful sedatives into her food and drink.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

That case, which concluded with sweeping convictions in December 2024, set a stark legal and cultural benchmark in France.

News.title: Shocking moment brawl erupts after woman blows vape at passengers on packed Tube
News.content:
THIS is the shocking moment a brawl erupted on a packed Tube after passengers tell a woman to stop vaping.

The footage, recorded on an Elizabeth line carriage, shows a shameless youngster puffing smoke towards others.





A man tells her “don’t smoke in the middle of the train” but she blows vape clouds into his face instead.

After repeated calls for her to stop, she tells him to “f*** off” and appears to spit on him.

The yob and her group of friends start shouting at other passengers and the altercation turns physical.

It appears punches are thrown as people desperately try to break up the brawl.

The youngsters continue shouting vile racial abuse at one man as they are forced onto the platform at Stratford.

A British Transport Police (BTP) spokesperson said: “We’re aware of a video showing an altercation on a train. Enquiries are ongoing. Anyone with information should text BTP on 61016, or call 0800 40 50 40, using the reference 134 of March 20.”

Smoking, including e-cigarettes and vapes, on TfL trains is punishable by fines of up to £1,000.

A TfL spokesperson added: “We prohibit smoking and vaping across all of our services and premises.



“Everyone has the right to travel safely and we encourage anyone who experiences or witnesses a crime or antisocial behaviour on the Tube or on our rail services to report it to the British Transport Police by texting 61016.”

This comes as Sadiq Khan has come under fire as he attempts a crackdown on crime in the capital.

Critics accused the London Mayor of being “out of touch” and sitting in an “ivory tower”.

Last year he spent £500,000 of public money in a rewilding project in Dagenham, East London, one of the capital’s poorest boroughs.

The money is part of a £3.84million pot of cash from Sir Sadiq’s Green Roots Fund which is aimed at boosting access to nature around London.



But the move comes as fares on the London Underground, the Overground and the Elizabeth Line will rise by 5.8 per cent.

The increase is 1% above the rate of inflation and comes into force this month.

Concerns about crime remains a big concern, with the latest figures showing the overall crime rate in London is 81 crimes per 1,000 residents.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

The most common crimes reported are violence and sexual offences.

Reports come from roughly every 23 out of 1,000 daytime population, according to Crime Rate.

News.title: Trump calls Nato allies ‘COWARDS’ in devastating attack over Strait of Hormuz as missing Ayatollah ‘gives new statement’
News.content:
DONALD Trump has slammed Nato allies as “cowards” in a fresh swipe at the defence pact over the deepening Strait of Hormuz crisis.

The missing Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei has also issued a new statement calling for Iran‘s enemies to be stripped of their “security” as the Middle East war spirals.





It comes after the US president called on his allies to help him unblock the key shipping route – as Iran‘s chokehold on it sends global oil prices spiralling.

Trump also said it would be “easy” for them to allow ships to pass safely through the critical trade artery.

Iran has blitzed ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz and maintained an effective blockade over it for weeks now.

The increasingly impatient US president said: “Without the U.S.A., NATO IS A PAPER TIGER! They didn’t want to join the fight to stop a Nuclear Powered Iran.

“Now that fight is Militarily WON, with very little danger for them, they complain about the high oil prices they are forced to pay, but don’t want to help open the Strait of Hormuz, a simple military maneuver that is the single reason for the high oil prices.

“So easy for them to do, with so little risk. COWARDS, and we will REMEMBER!”

The Strait of Hormuz is a vital trade route and passage for around 20 per cent of the world’s oil.

Meanwhile, Iran’s missing Supreme Leader has issued a new statement on the war with the US and Israel.



The Ayatollah called on Tehran to ignore the security of Iran’s “internal and external enemies”.

Mojtaba, who is reportedly in a coma and missing one leg, has not been seen publicly since his late father’s assassination on February 28.

In the fresh statement, Mojtaba also called his dad’s assassination a “tragedy of martyrdom” and claimed attacks throughout the war on Oman and Turkey were not carried out by Iran.

It comes after Britain sent senior military officers to the US earlier this week to help draw up plans to reopen the strait.

Despite this, Royal Navy ships are not expected to be deployed to the region to escort vessels through.



European nations including France and Germany said on Thursday they were ready to join “appropriate efforts” to ensure safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz.

Israel also struck the South Pars gasfield on Wednesday – prompting Iran to ramp up its own attacks sending energy prices soaring.

Speaking at an awards ceremony on Friday, Trump reiterated that the US was doing “extremely well”.



He said his forces had knocked down 58 ships in two days, declaring Tehran had “no navy anymore”.

Iran has also told the UK that allowing US forces to use British bases will be considered “participation in aggression”.

Downing Street has said it will “not get drawn into the wider war”.

It also comes amid reports that Trump is mulling plans to send in troops to seize Iran’s vital oil processing island in a dramatic bid to free blockaded shipping.

The US president is considering a bold move to occupy or seal off Iran’s Kharg Island – which processes 90 per cent of the rogue state’s crude.



More than 2,500 US marines are closing in on the war zone as the defiant mullahs continue to ramp up global economic chaos by attacking their neighbours’ energy plants.

But analysts warned putting boots on the ground on the island – 15 miles off the Iranian coast – would be a huge gamble risking massive casualties.

Four US sources confirmed on Friday Trump is weighing up the option of a ground invasion.



RECOMMENDED STORIES

A source with knowledge of White House thinking told Axios.”We need about a month to weaken the Iranians more with strikes, take the island and then get them by the balls and use it for negotiations.”

The US said on Friday it has deployed Apache helicopters and low-flying jets to blitz Iranian ships near the strategically important waterway.

News.title: Students risk spreading meningitis across the UK during Easter holidays, experts warn – as thousands return home
News.content:
FEARS are growing that uni students could spread the killer meningitis bug across Britain as thousands head home this Easter.

Health bosses warned the outbreak at the University of Kent could spark “clusters” nationwide, with some students potentially already infected before travelling.





Cases have surged to 29, according to the UK Health Security Agency, with at least one patient now being treated in London.

Tragically, two students have died – including a 21-year-old from the University of Kent, and an 18-year-old sixth form student from Kent, Juliette Kenny.

Michael Kenny, Juliette’s heartbroken father, today called for urgent action to better protect young people.



Worryingly, Dr Anjan Ghosh, Kent County Council’s director of public health, said it was “possible” infections could spread beyond the county as students leave campus.

He revealed 12 of those seriously ill in hospital are University of Kent students, while at least one other case is linked to neighbouring Canterbury Christ Church University.

And officials fear the number could still rise.

Dr Ghosh warned it was too early to say whether the outbreak had peaked and outlined three possible scenarios over the coming weeks.



“The first scenario is it stays more or less contained in Kent,” he said.

“[The] second scenario is that there are people who have left and gone off campus.

“They were incubating when they left and they become cases of small household sporadic clusters outside Kent.

“The third scenario is the worst-case scenario, which is that those cases which happened out of Kent [create] a cluster of cases outside Kent.”

When asked who would be most vulnerable in these households, Dr Ghosh said: “The people who are more vulnerable are always people with underlying health conditions.



“And yes, those who are the youngest and those who are the eldest are most vulnerable, in general.

“But we have a vaccination programme for younger kids, they are less vulnerable.

“It is basically again 18-plus age group onwards and older people often have underlying conditions which make them vulnerable.”

He said the “most likely scenario” is that it is contained in Kent with a few additional cases outside of Kent “which can be easily contained”.

What is meningitis and what are the symptoms? Meningitis is an infection of the protective membranes that surround the brain and spinal cord – called the meninges. It can be very serious if not treated quickly – it can cause life-threatening sepsis and result in permanent damage to the brain or nerves. Symptoms include: A high temperature (fever)

Being sick

A headache

A rash that does not fade when a glass is rolled over it (but a rash will not always develop)

A stiff neck

A dislike of bright lights

Drowsiness or unresponsiveness

Seizures (fits) Call 999 for an ambulance or go to your nearest A&E immediately if you think you or someone you look after could have meningitis or sepsis. Meningitis is usually caused by a bacterial or viral infection. Bacterial meningitis is rarer but more serious than viral meningitis. Vaccinations offer some protection against certain causes of meningitis. These include the: MenB vaccine – offered to babies aged 8 weeks, followed by a second dose at 16 weeks and a booster at 1 year

6-in-1 vaccine – offered to babies at 8, 12 and 16 weeks of age

pneumococcal vaccine – 2 doses offered to babies at 12 weeks and 1 year, and a single dose offered to adults aged 65 or over

Hib/MenC vaccine – offered to babies at 1 year of age

MMR vaccine – offered to babies at 1 year and a second dose at 3 years and 4 months

MenACWY vaccine – offered to teenagers, sixth formers and “fresher” students going to university for the first time Source: NHS



“Because a lot of the cases come from University of Kent students resident in the campus, who now have broken up for holidays and Easter breaks,” he explained.

“So theoretically you could have someone who was incubating at that time not showing symptoms and then going off on holiday home, and basically going out sort of Kent, many of the students live outside of Kent.

“And then they start to show symptoms and then with their close contacts potentially spread it, even if that was to happen that would be a very limited to household kind of thing which can be easily managed.”

Meningitis can take between two and 10 days to show symptoms.



This means that infected students may not yet realise that they are carrying the killer disease.

Health officials are now racing to find out whether a mutated strain is behind the spike.

At least 13 cases have been confirmed as meningitis B.

Early analysis suggests the bug is similar to existing strains, with current vaccines still effective, but experts have not ruled out mutation.

A mass vaccination drive has been launched in a desperate bid to contain the outbreak.



It has now been expanded to include revellers who visited Canterbury nightclub Club Chemistry – thought to be at the centre of the spread – as well as sixth-form pupils, and university students and staff.

Earlier this week, The Sun revealed 21-year-old Keeleigh Goodwin is believed to have become infected after sharing a vape at Club Chemistry.

NHS bosses have scrambled to secure more jabs, requesting 5,000 extra doses after already receiving 6,500.

This week has seen huge queues form at vaccination centres, with more than 1,000 students lining up at the University of Kent on Friday alone.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

More than 2,500 people have already received the jab, while over 10,000 doses of antibiotics have been handed out.



Students who have already returned home have been urged to contact their GP for a free vaccine.

News.title: Moment cop screams in agony as he is BITTEN by thug, 39, during arrest hours after being freed from prison
News.content:
THIS is the shocking moment a cop screams in agony as he’s bitten by a thug hours after being freed from prison.

Dramatic footage shows yob Luke Goodhall, 39, being cuffed and restrained on the ground by officers.





A cop shouts: “Stop kicking your legs out. Don’t punch. Do not bite me, get off my leg.”

He yells in disbelief “you’re biting me” before screaming out in agony.

The officer tells Goodhall: “You’re further under arrest for assaulting a police officer.”



But the thug laughs and says: “no-one’s sin (sic) it you d***head”.

The brutal encounter unfolded on February 17, at around 7.20pm on Eastgate Street in Gloucester.

Goodhall was spotted on CCTV running after a person who he later allegedly assaulted.

Gloucestershire Police tracked him down and detained him before the violent scenes escalated.



Goodhall, from Cheltenham, was later charged and at Cheltenham Magistrates Court last month and admitted two offences.

He was sentenced to nine months in prison for assaulting the officer.

A concurrent term of six months was added for common assault of an emergency worker at the scene.

He was also charged with one count of assault by beating another person and is due in court on March 25 in relation to this offence.

Goodhall assaulted the officers while he was on licence which means he will also have to carry out the rest of his previous sentence behind bars.



Before the disturbing bodycam footage was released, another video filmed by a member of the public, had been posted on social media.

It showed a Gloucestershire Police officer detaining a man in the street and sparked controversy that unnecessary force had been used.

But the clip, which was posted without sound, did not show Goodhall biting the officer’s leg.

A spokesperson for the force said: ”A man has been jailed after he bit a police officer during his arrest in Gloucester last month.

”Gloucestershire Police’s Professional Standards Department reviewed the bodyworn video and the use of force by the officer (strikes to the body) has been deemed proportionate, necessary and legitimate.



”Police have said they understand videos like this, where there is use of force, can cause concern but often the whole story is not known or captured.

“He was further arrested for biting the officer and for trying to knee another officer in the face during a search.”

And, the bitten police officer, who has served the force for 11 years, said: “I am now relieved that the full incident is being disclosed to promote that healthy discussion and hopefully bring about a better understanding of what occurred within this incident.

“It is unfortunate that I was injured in this instance, but if any positive can be drawn from this, I hope the transparency helps foster an understanding into use of force and decision making.

“I believe I have a good rapport with the people I come into contact with and the people of Gloucester; this is what I aimed to do when I started policing.”



Temporary Chief Superintendent Erica Field, Gloucestershire Police’s Use of Force lead, said officers are “only allowed to use force to the extent that it is necessary, proportionate and legitimate in all the circumstances”.

“We will not tolerate any assault on our officers and will always seek a prosecution,” she added.

Temporary Inspector Adam Williams, Branch Chair of Gloucestershire Police Federation, continued: “In this incident, the full body-worn video confirms our colleague acted reasonably, proportionately, and entirely in line with his training while being violently assaulted.

“He was bitten during the struggle, causing a genuine injury, and he responded only to protect himself and bring a dangerous situation under control.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

The force highlighted nearly 49,000 officers have been assaulted in the past year.



This equates to an officer being attacked every 10 minutes in the UK.

News.title: Dark curse of the Dreamboys from horrific axe attack & shotgun murder to bizarre stripping accident ‘that BLINDED a fan’
News.content:
THEY are the oil-drenched, muscle mountains who leave thousands of women a week screaming in ecstasy while watching their sexy stripteases.

But when the curtains fall, there is a darker side to some of the hunky hotties who make up the Dreamboys – including some who have been found guilty of shocking crimes.





This week, ex-stripper George Goodayle turned himself in to French police after his girlfriend Jayne Gregory, 49, was found dead with two gunshot wounds and a firearm beside her body.

Goodayle, from Peterborough, has been charged with spousal murder but claims the weapon went off accidentally and is being held in pre-trial detention.

The former Dreamboy, who used to dance under the name ‘Gorgeous George’, had been dating Gregory for six months and they lived together in Brillac, south-west France.

Goodayle, who denies murder, isn’t the only stripper from the troupe to find themselves in trouble with the law or facing public condemnation over their dubious choices.

The sheer number of ex-members embroiled in scandal and suffering premature deaths have led some to even question if there is a ‘Dreamboys curse’.

Among the most horrific was David Richards, who was jailed for 27 years after attempting to kill his estranged-wife in 2023.

The former Dreamboys boss, who sold the business for £1million in 2019, ambushed his ex Alex Aslam with an axe, inflicting cuts and three fractures to her skull, which required more than 100 stitches.

The attack followed Richards being kicked out of their home and reported to police for coercive control and domestic abuse. He waited outside the property for hours for her to return from a date.



He spotted Alex was accompanied by TOWIE star Kirk Norcross, to whom she gave a ‘goodnight kiss’ on the doorstep, and after he left, Richards struck.

Terrified Alex, who was “covered in blood”, grabbed a kitchen knife to defend herself and managed to lock herself in the bathroom.

There she video called Kirk, who she begged to rescue her children, and wailed while bleeding profusely: “I am dying. He has killed me, he has done me.”



In court, Alex described the scene as a “bloodbath” and like a “horror movie” and Richards, 42, was found guilty of attempted murder in January 2023.

Two months later he was found dead in his cell at HMP Lowdham Grange, near Nottingham, having taking his own life.

£200k scam

At the height of his fame in the 90s, self-professed “king of male strip tease shows” Barry Solomons partied with Spice Girls, met Princess Diana and was rarely seen without a glamorous lady on his arm.



But by 2016, the Dreamboys founder, then 71, and his business partner Laurel Goodman were revealed to have conned the taxpayers out of £405,000 through benefits fraud and tax evasion.

Solomons, who used to strip under the name ‘Bari Bacco’, evaded paying £38,000 in income tax for the eight years until 2013 and also claimed housing benefit for two separate homes in Islington, north-east London.

Meanwhile Goodman had racked up a staggering £122,000 in taxpayer handouts including housing benefit, income support and council tax benefits.

In addition to that, she deprived the taxman of £171,000 despite having earned more than £621,000 between 2007 and 2013.

Prosecutor Andrew Evans said: “It was the bare faced cheek of projecting themselves as established members of the industry while taking money from the public purse.”



Solomons was jailed for 28 months but Goodman, then 55, went on the run after leaving her lawyer a note stating: “I am going somewhere where I am safe and cannot be found.”

She evaded capture for a year after moving to Wells-next-the-Sea, Norfolk, where she set up the new identity Chassie Tatum – a name strikingly similar to Channing Tatum, the actor who starred in 2012 stripper film Magic Mike. She was also jailed for 28 months.

TV scandal

During his Dreamboy days, Lotan Carter was so worried about getting injured by sex-mad ladies that he insured his penis for a staggering £12million.



It followed a colleague having to take two weeks off work when a shrieking punter “grabbed his tackle a bit too hard” during a show and injured him.

Lotan, who is the nephew of TV dancer Louie Spence, was the victim of revenge porn in 2015 when screenshots of him pleasuring himself during a “sexy Skype session” were leaked.

After that incident, he had a short-lived experience on Big Brother, lasting just four weeks before being kicked out for “intimidating behaviour”.

It followed the Dreamboy chucking his drink over three female housemates and chucking a bottle across the room. The ensuing drama had to be broken up by security.

Lotan, whose outburst followed his behaviour being challenged by housemates, later complained: “F*** the TV show, f*** the situation and f*** the girls who went off.”



Tragic death

Prior to his death in 2017, muscly Ghent Wakefield, 35, certainly lived a colourful life.

For a time, he was a soldier, a wrestler, a Dreamboy, and most shockingly a ‘henchman’ and chauffeur to a student crook.

Wakefield bizarrely served criminal Paul Hackney, who he would drive to university in a Rolls Royce Phantom, and got involved in his illegal activities.

Through Hackney, he was embroiled in a £1.5m scam that fraudulently claimed back VAT from Revenue and Customs after buying cars overseas, prior to Brexit.



In 2013, Wakefield, of Staffordshire, was jailed for 17 months and after his release tried to become a WWE wrestler, only for his career to be cut short by a ruptured bicep.

The muscle man, who was a known steroid user, was discovered dead in his room, inside a shared house, by his landlord in 2017 after neighbours grew concerned.

It was known that Wakefield was using a dangerous mix of the bulk-increasing drug and insulin together to increase his size.

An inquest couldn’t conclude the cause of death but the coroner noted the “strongest possibility is the abuse of insulin”.

‘Blinding furore’



Grandma Merle Groenewald alleged that she was blinded after a stripper, dressed as a fireman, “ripped off his clothes” and accidentally whipped her with his trousers.

She sued Dreamboys London for £150,000 in 2019 after claiming the firm failed to ensure there was “sufficient space between the performers and the audience” resulting in the injury.

The hotel worker, who said her “knees were touching the stage” at the Leicester Square venue in 2014, “lost useful vision in her left eye” and was temporarily blinded in her right.

Dreamboys London argued they were not at fault and said an accident involving a stripper’s trousers was not “reasonably foreseeable”.

Their barrister continued: “She complained of having suffered some accidental injury to one of her eyes, but no admission is made as to whether, or if so how, any such injury occurred”.



The case was set for a three-day trial in 2019. At the time, churchgoer Merle, originally from South Africa, said: “It’s in God’s hands now.” The outcome of the case has not been reported.

Bloody injuries

Being fawned over by hundreds of titillated women at every show may seem like a dream to some fellas, but Dreamboy dancers previously told The Sun there are hazards of the job.

Birmingham-based Pjay Finch, who appeared on TV show Married At First Sight, claimed excessively boozed-up ladies get carried away by scratching, inappropriately touching and even biting them.

He says: “I’m like, ‘Come on, this is a professional show, and we don’t want to leave with bite marks on our bum cheeks.’”

Pjay also recalls a deeply uncomfortable moment when a woman began aggressively “playing with herself” during one show while staring at him. He says she was “sick all over herself moments later”.



He’s also been groped and grabbed without consent, leading him to say: “If this was the other way round and I was doing the same thing to a woman, I would be arrested.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Similarly Rogan O’Connor, from Stratford-upon-Avon, recalled coming off stage to realise “there was blood all down my stomach and legs”.

He then noticed masses of claw marks on his abs from one especially rowdy fan.

News.title: Terrifying moment train derails and crashes leaving one dead and seven people seriously injured in horror smash
News.content:
THIS is the terrifying moment a train derails off the tracks in a cloud of dust leaving one person dead and seven seriously injured.

Footage of the horror smash showed carriage after carriage veering off course and down an embankment.





The video shows the train screeching to a halt with at least a dozen freight coaches careening off the tracks.

The freight train was heading towards the northern state of Coahuila in Mexico on Thursday morning.

The locomotive, operated by the railway operator Ferromex, was reportedly transporting 10,900 tons of steel at the time.



It derailed near the town of Rincón de Romos near a local restaurant, causing immense property damage.

One person was killed and at least seven were injured.

The deceased has been identified as Honduran national José Ricard, 40.

He was reportedly trying to make his way to the border to try to cross into the US.



Among the injured was Isidro, 45, who needs his left leg amputated after the crash.

They were allegedly riding on top of the carriages at the time.

No casualties were reported among the train crew.

Following a preliminary investigation, it’s believed that the theft of railway sleepers from the track was the main cause of the crash.

However, a mechanical failure has not yet been ruled out.



A full investigation into the derailment is ongoing.

News.title: MAGA TV star Tucker Carlson praises Britain's most notorious fascist in deranged video
News.content:
MAGA TV star Tucker Carlson praises Britain's most notorious fascist in deranged video

In the video Tucker Carlson, a former Fox News host and, until the US war in Iran, a long-time ally of Donald Trump, gushes over Oswald Mosley, the leader of the British Union of Fascists in the 1930s

View Image Tucker Carlson sang the praises of Oswald Mosley (Image: AP)

Right-wing US TV host Tucker Carlson released a video praising Britain's most notorious wartime fascist - and attacking Winston Churchill

‌



In the video Carlson, a former Fox News host and, until the US war in Iran, a long-time ally of Donald Trump, gushes over Oswald Mosley, the leader of the British Union of Fascists in the 1930s.

‌



Mosley was interned by the British Government for three years along with his wife in the early 40s, over concerns about his Nazi sympathies.

‌



The clip, posted to Twitter overnight, sees Carlson claim Mosley was the "leader of the opposition", which he was not, and that he was imprisoned for the "entire length of the war", which he was not.

He was held in HMP Brixton in 1940 before being moved to Holloway where he stayed until 1943. He was released on health grounds two years before the war ended.

Content cannot be displayed without consent

‌



In the video, Carlson claims: "Winston Churchill, who I know we're required to deify, presided over the imprisonment of his opposition party during the entire length of the war -- and their families, and their wives! They're rotting in prison, away from their little kids, in some cases their infants. And their crime was being the opposition party, and being disloyal and unpatriotic. They weren't. The opposition party was led by a First World War war hero who fought not just as, you know, a pilot in the sky, but and in the trenches, like one of the great war heroes, former member of parliament, the country ever produced.

"And he and his wife and his compatriots and their wives were interned without charges by Winston Churchill for the duration of the war. And that happened in Britain which is like much more humane than a lot of places. FDR interned the Japanese including American citizens. That stuff happens during warm and so I think we should be on guard."

Mosley was not a member of the "opposition party" - he'd left Parliament nearly a decade earlier to become the leader of the British Union of Fascists.

‌



Neither was he a WWI "hero" - originally a pilot, he was injured in a plane crash while showing off for his mother and sister, and spent the rest of the war in a desk job.

In 1936, he got married in Joseph Goebbels' home, at a ceremony attended by Adolf Hitler.

Once a stalwart Trump supporter, has drifted to the right in recent years - dabbling further in conspiracy theories.

‌



He remains influential among conservatives, despite being denounced for hosting Nick Fuentes, a white nationalist and antisemite, on his podcast last year. During the interview, Fuentes complained about "organized Jewry in America."

Conservatives are still reckoning with the fallout from Carlson’s interview with Fuentes.

For example, board members and other staff members resigned from the Heritage Foundation after the think tank's president defended Carlson.

‌



Trump tried to sidestep the issue, declining to criticise Fuentes and praising Carlson for having “said good things about me over the years.” The president previously dined with Fuentes at his Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach, Florida, between his two terms, and Carlson has continued to visit the White House.

Mort Klein, president of the conservative Jewish group the Zionist Organization of America, said Wednesday that he supports Trump but “I’d like him to do more” about antisemitism.

“I want him to be stronger on those issues,” Klein said.

‌



Carlson has said that he is not antisemitic. But he has said that anti-Jewish hate is less pervasive in society than bias against white people and that some Christian politicians who were fervent supporters of Israel were guilty of heresy.

The Iran war is poised to continue fracturing right-wing media.

Ben Shapiro, co-founder of The Daily Wire, called Carlson’s Fuentes interview “an act of moral imbecility” and accused the host of misleading his audience with falsehoods and conspiracy theories.

‌



He's also feuded with Candace Owens, who has promoted antisemitic conspiracy theories. Dennis Prager, a conservative commentator, wrote in an open letter to Owens that “I cannot think of anyone in public life engendering as much suspicion of Jews, Zionism and Israel as you.”

Megyn Kelly, like Carlson a former Fox News Channel anchor now helming her own independent media empire, said the war was sold to the American people by “Israel firsters, like Mark Levin.” Levin, a radio and Fox personality, has been among Trump’s most fervent supporters of the war.

Levin, for his part, called Kelly an “emotionally unhinged, lewd and petulant wreck.”

News.title: Muriel McKay search breakthrough as bone found in garden 55 years after disappearing
News.content:
A major breakthrough in the case of Muriel McKay, who was kidnapped and murdered 55 years ago, has been shared by her family.

A nine-inch bone was discovered at around 1.45pm today, Mrs McKay's grandson Mary Dyer revealed. He added that the bone appeared to have been "sliced with a weapon or machine".

A Metropolitan Police forensics team arrived at the scene on Friday to analyse the bone, which is a couple of inches wide.

The discovery was made about a metre down in the back yard of a betting shop on the Bethnal Green Road, in Hackney, East London.

The Met said in a statement: "Police are aware of reports surrounding the discovery of a single bone in the garden of a property in Bethnal Green Road, Hackney. The bone was uncovered during an independent search.

"Officers are now on scene and work is being undertaken to establish the origin."

Mr Dyer said: "It would be a great outcome to end this ghastly mystery for our whole family and all of those who've been interested enough to follow our story."

The search was mounted after former Kray Twins tailor Percy Chaplin's children said he suspected Mrs McKay was buried in the yard of his former shop. Arthur Hosein, one of the two brothers who killed the 55-year-old, was working there at the time.

He and sibling Nizamodeen were jailed for life for the murder after a bungled kidnap in which they mistook Muriel for Australian media tycoon Rupert Murdoch's then wife Anna.

Nizamodeen has told her family's lawyer that he buried her on the Hertfordshire farm where they held her captive, but a search of the area in 2022 found nothing.

This is a breaking news story.

News.title: Andrew 'still using HRH' on moving boxes despite King Charles stripping royal title
News.content:
Andrew 'still using HRH' on moving boxes despite King Charles stripping royal title

As the former prince Andrew prepares to move into his permanent home of Marsh Farm, workers for a removal company were spotted carrying boxes that feature a title that was stripped by the late Queen in 2022

View 4 Images Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor was stripped of all his royal titles by King Charles last year (Image: AFP via Getty Images)

Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor is preparing to move from his temporary home of Wood Farm into his permanent lodgings of Marsh Farm on the Sandringham estate.

Royal sources believe the move is imminent, after three lorries transporting fine art were spotted driving to Marsh Farm on Tuesday. Now, a group of removalists have been seen on the Norfolk estate carrying a series of boxes.

The boxes in question are labelled ‘HRH’ - or His Royal Highness - followed by the room the contents are destined for. Andrew was stripped from using his HRH title by Queen Elizabeth in 2022, when his military titles and royal patronages were returned to the late Queen.

Netflix boss quietly unfollows Meghan Markle amid claims of ‘difficult’ relationship READ MORE :

View 4 Images Moving boxes at Sandringham have been seen with an HRH label (Image: Bav Media)

The boxes being unloaded has signified to many that Andrew’s move into his more permanent home is just around the corner. Earlier this week, three large lorries from Gander & White - an art logistics company with a royal warrant - were seen arriving at Marsh Farm, near Wolferton, believed to be carrying paintings.

The company, which has the slogan "custodians of the irreplaceable", specialises in transporting fine art and has worked for prestigious clients, including the National Portrait Gallery in London. Since being booted out of Royal Lodge earlier this year, the ex-prince has been living temporarily at Wood Farm - a small cottage, where his father the late Prince Philip lived out his final days.

Mountbatten-Windsor has been holed up inside the cottage ever since he was pictured slouched in the back of a car leaving a Norfolk Police Station following his arrest on suspicion of misconduct in a public office.

View 4 Images Andrew was barred from using the HRH title by the late Queen in 2022 (Image: Bav Media)

Andrew was forced to give up the lease of his former Windsor mansion Royal Lodge after being stripped of all his royal titles in October last year following the latest revelations about his association with billionaire paedophile Jeffrey Epstein.

There was also a huge outcry after it emerged he had only been paying a peppercorn rent for the mansion, which has prompted an investigation into the Crown Estate into why he had been handed such a lucrative lease.

It is believed that at Mountbatten-Windsor's former Royal Lodge home, various historic paintings and artefact decorated the walls of the mansion. However, much of the art adorning the walls belongs to the Royal Collection Trust, meaning Andrew is unable to remove it from the Windsor home.

View 4 Images Andrew will soon be moving to Marsh Farm on the Sandringham estate (Image: AP)

For several months, Marsh Farm has been undergoing major renovations to prepare for Andrew’s arrival. In January engineers from Sky were seen scaling ladders installing what is thought to be TV and broadband ready for the former prince to move in.

Electricians were also spotted working at the property with extra security, including CCTV, also installed. It is around seven miles from the main Sandringham House and a no-fly zone over the royal estate has been extended to include the home.

Article continues below

Marsh Farm has a main house, but the two reception rooms and kitchen are said to be undergoing major renovations. A huge six-foot security fence has also been thrown up around the property.

News.title: Ex-Sinn Fein president Gerry Adams has IRA bombing claims DROPPED in 'emphatic end'
News.content:
Ex-Sinn Fein president Gerry Adams has IRA bombing claims DROPPED in 'emphatic end'

Three victims of Provisional IRA bombings in England have stopped their damages claim against former Sinn Fein president Gerry Adams

View 2 Images Gerry Adams says the case against him should never have been brought. (Image: PA)

The civil case against Gerry Adams over IRA bombing has been withdrawn.

‌



Adams welcomed the "emphatic end" of the High Court damages claim against him. He said the case - by three victims of Provisional IRA bombings - "should never have been brought".

‌



On Friday, the final day of a two-week trial, lawyers for the victims said that the claim against the former Sinn Fein president had been "discontinued" with "no order as to costs." The claimants’ lawyer, Anne Studd KC, was expected to finish her closing submissions on Friday, but instead told the high court that the claim would be discontinued after “proceedings developed overnight”.

‌



The victims who brought the case alleged that Adams, 77, was personally liable for injuries they received in IRA attacks in London and Manchester in 1973 and 1996. The former Sinn Féin leader had insisted he had no role in the explosions and strongly denied a claim that he was a "major player" in the IRA.

The case was brought by John Clark, a victim of the 1973 Old Bailey bombing in London; Jonathan Ganesh, a 1996 London Docklands bombing victim; and Barry Laycock, a victim of the 1996 Arndale shopping centre bombing in Manchester.

View 2 Images Former Sinn Fein president Gerry Adams arriving at the Royal Courts of Justice, in central London for the civil claim against him (Image: PA)

‌



People on PIP never need to report any of these six changes to the DWP READ MORE :

The trio were suing Mr Adams for a symbolic £1 in damages. The allegations were that he was a leading member of the Provisional IRA on those dates, including of its army council.

The claimants raised more than £100,000 through crowdfunding to bring the case and said they were acting not just for themselves, but for all IRA victims. But Mr Adams told the court in London that he had "no involvement whatsoever" in the bombings and was never a member of the Provisional IRA, with his lawyers claiming that the case should be thrown out as an abuse of the court system.

In a statement following Friday's announcement, Mr Adams said he attended the trial "out of respect" for the victims and to defend himself "against the smears and false accusations being levelled against me".

Article continues below

He said: "I asserted the legitimacy of the Republican cause and the right of the people of Ireland to freedom and self-determination. I do so again. During my two days of evidence, I categorically rejected all of the claims being made.I am glad to have been one of those who helped bring an end to the conflict. We now have, through the Good Friday Agreement, a peaceful and democratic route to a new Ireland. That needs a renewed focus, especially by the Irish government.

"An Ireland that is respectful of all of its people and that is based on equality, tolerance and respect. I want to thank all of those who have expressed their solidarity with me and the Sinn Fein team which worked closely with me."

Due to the result of a pre-trial ruling, Adams is unable to recover his legal costs from the claimants, believed to be six figures.

News.title: Lip reader says King Charles issued brutal response to Prince Harry complaint
News.content:
Lip reader says King Charles issued brutal response to Prince Harry complaint

Prince Harry and Prince William were on a skiing holiday with their father, now King Charles, in the Rocky Mountains when the young prince made a fashion faux pas

View 3 Images Charles waved to the crowds as Harry sulked (Image: Getty)

During his first holiday with his father and brother following the sudden, devastating death of his mother Princess Diana, Prince Harry made the sort of schoolboy error that any 13-year-old lad might make, choosing fashion over function and sporting his favourite baseball cap on the freezing slopes of an exclusive Rocky Mountains ski resort. But unlike most 13-year-olds, Harry's misjudgement unfolded before the world's media, with cameras documenting the moment during what was already a profoundly emotional time for the young royal.

‌



And now, courtesy of a team of expert lip-readers who have examined archive footage of that significant moment in his life, we can discover precisely what was said that day too.

‌



Speaking Channel 5's Lip-Reading the Royals: What Are They Really Saying?, forensic lip-reader Nicola Hickling disclosed the exchange Harry and Prince William had with their father, the King — then the Prince of Wales — as a ski lift transported them to the mountain's summit. She says that Harry appeared to tell his father: "I'm so cold."

‌



But rather than attempting to comfort Harry, Charles instead turned to William and, according to Nicola, said: "He has no sense. Of course it's bloody cold. We're in the snow."

View 3 Images The trio posed for photos in the sub-zero remperatures (Image: Getty)

Then William, seemingly addressing Charles as though his brother wasn't present, appears to ask: "Didn't he bring a spare hat?" According to Nicola, Charles's indifferent response was: "He wanted to wear that cap. I told him he was mad and that it's very cold. He doesn't make it easy for himself,” at which point William chimed in with his own dismissive comment: "Let him crack on. He will learn. He will probably get frostbite."

‌



Charles seems to conclude the conversation by nonchalantly saying: "Perhaps."

Reflecting on the lip-reader's interpretation, actress Nina Wadia remarks: "Well of course style is more important than practicality - he's a teenage boy! Of course he's going to wear a baseball cap on a ski lift and then complain that it's cold."

She continues: "I love the fact that his brother and his father are ripping into him."

‌



Many years later, William would encounter the opposite problem when he found himself somewhat overdressed for the 41ºC (106F) heat outside the iconic Taj Mahal in India, offering a stark contrast to the earlier scene on the freezing slopes.

View 3 Images Poor Harry was suffering as they rode the ski lift (Image: Getty)

The lip-readers believe that the Prince complained to his wife, Princess Catherine, about how hot it was, telling her that he wished he hadn't worn his rather formal blue sports jacket during the visit.

Article continues below

When Catherine suggested he might take it off, William appeared to respond: "Are kidding? I don't think anything I wear would cool me down."

Nina observes that these revealing exchanges help to humanise the royals, making it clear that beneath all the pomp and ceremony, they are just like the rest of us.

The reporting on the royals' Canadian holiday in British Columbia features in Episode 4 of Lip-Reading the Royals, which broadcasts at 8pm on Saturday, March 21.

News.title: Meghan Markle brutally mocked by Comic Relief host over Prince Harry comment
News.content:
Meghan Markle brutally mocked by Comic Relief host over Prince Harry comment

Comic Relief: Funny For Money star Joel Dommett took a swipe at Meghan Markle's past comments about Prince Harry, while opening up about his own marriage

View 3 Images Prince Harry and Meghan Markle married in 2018 (Image: Getty)

Comic Relief host Joel Dommett has taken a playful jab at Meghan Markle's previous remarks about her husband, Prince Harry. In 2021, the Duchess of Sussex claimed she knew little about Harry or royal protocols prior to their relationship, despite his prominent status. As she became more acquainted with him on a personal level, Meghan also asserted she hadn't used Google to learn more about him.

‌



In conversation with Oprah Winfrey, she stated: "Well, I didn't do any research about what that would mean... I'd never looked up my husband online. I just didn't feel a need to because everything that I needed to know, he was sharing with me, right? Everything that he thought I needed to know, he was telling me."

‌



In the pair's Netflix documentary released a year later, a resurfaced 2015 clip showed Meghan being questioned about Harry and his brother Prince William at a red carpet event.

‌



When asked who she preferred out of the two, she selected Harry - whom she would later wed in 2018. Discussing the start of his own relationship with his wife Hannah, Joel couldn't resist alluding to Meghan's comments while speaking on Davina McCall's Begin Again podcast.

View 3 Images Meghan Markle previously said she didn't look up her husband Prince Harry (Image: Getty)

He quipped that his wife professed she didn't know who he was after messaging him, following an appearance on Lorraine Kelly's show.

‌



"My wife slid into my DMs. The TV was on in the corner of the bar and she saw me on Lorraine," he shared. "She hadn't seen any of the junk [about a previous catfishing situation he fell victim to]."

He then joked: "That same way that Meghan Markle didn't know Prince Harry - 'Oh, what is a Prince?'".

Joel, 40, wed Hannah in Mykonos, Greece, three years after they met. The pair share a son, Wilde, and are currently expecting their second child.

‌



Harry and Meghan, meanwhile, have two children together - Archie and Lilibet. The duo stepped back from senior royal duties in 2020 before discussing their decision to depart in their high-profile interview with Oprah.

View 3 Images Joel Dommett with his wife Hannah (Image: Getty)

They subsequently released a six-part Netflix documentary providing a more personal glimpse into their relationship, from how they met to their exit from royal life.

‌



Harry additionally elaborated on his experiences as a royal in his memoir, Spare. Since 2020, the pair have been living in the US, making only occasional visits to the UK.

Meanwhile, Joel returns to screens tonight as one of the presenters for this year's Comic Relief Red Nose Day.

The BBC One programme will feature an evening of entertainment, with appearances from stars including Davina, Joel, Katherine Ryan, Nick Mohammed and Catherine Tate.

Article continues below

Comic Relief: Funny For Money airs tonight at 7pm on BBC One.

News.title: Meningitis teen Juliette Kenny's dad reveals daughter's heartbreaking last moments
News.content:
Meningitis teen Juliette Kenny's dad reveals daughter's heartbreaking last moments

Michael Kenny said in an update that his teen daughter 'fought bravely for hours' before she died from meningitis last weekend, with cases ticking up after they exploded in Kent

View 4 Images Juliette Kenny sadly died from meningitis last week

Juliette Kenny's grieving dad has revealed what his grieving daughter went through in the final moments before she became one of the first casualties of the Kent meningitis outbreak.

‌



Juliette, 18, a year 13 pupil at Queen Elizabeth’s Grammar School in Faversham, died on Saturday, March 14, less than 24 hours after she contracted meningitis B. She is one of two people, including an unnamed 21-year-old student, who have died after catching the deadly bacterial infection.

‌



As the number of cases climb towards 30, Juliette's family has revealed his beloved daughter's final moments, with her dad saying the teen stayed brave until the end.

‌



Meningitis fears: Footage shows hundreds queuing in desperate hope of vaccine READ MORE :

View 4 Images Juliette was rushed to medics after she started appearing unwell (Image: PA)

Juliette's dad Michael said the family first noted something was wrong when she started vomiting and had discolouration to her cheeks on Friday, with his "fit, healthy and strong" daughter exibiting no other symptoms. They rushed her to medics immediately after she fell ill, who provided her with antibiotics before sending her on an ambulance ride to A&E.

Mr Kenny said: "In the morning on Friday, she was taken, by us, to our local emergency drop-in as a discolouration appeared on her cheeks. She was given antibiotics and sent by ambulance to A&E.

‌



"Juliette fought bravely for hours, but despite the fantastic NHS hospital staff fighting alongside her, meningitis took her from us less than 12 hours later. We were with her at the end, and the last sounds that she heard were the voices of those who loved her telling her how very much loved and cherished she is."

View 4 Images Juliette's dad called for the UK's vaccine programme to be extended (Image: Getty Images)

The dad said he and his wife are "incredibly proud parents", adding that Juliette is a "force in this world " whose death has caused the family "devastation". He said: "We are incredibly proud parents to two wonderful daughters.

‌



"Juliette is a force in this world. With her beautifully positive energy, she spread fun, love and happiness to those around her and she continues to do so now. The devastation of her loss to us, her family and friends is immeasurable.

"Sharing stories of the empathy, warmth and fun that she created is helping her family and friends through this time. Her energy continues to make the people who love her find a way."

View 4 Images Thousands of vaccines have been given out today alone (Image: PA)

Article continues below

Juliette's family has called on the government to widen the UKs vaccine programme after figures showed that 29 people have so far caught the bacteria since the outbreak began last weekend. NHS Kent and Medway has massively stepped up its vaccine programme, with around 4,514 provided with doses in the region today alone.

The integrated care system added in its release today that it has also passed out around 10,561 antibiotics. Officials in Kent had previously said that they had around 6,000 doses available for the public, and they have ordered an additional 5,000 as the bid to slow the outbreak continues.

News.title: UK shoppers given 'serious' warning as 'half of online reviews are fake'
News.content:
UK shoppers given 'serious' warning as 'half of online reviews are fake'

A five-year study has found that consumers are being misled on an industrial scale

View 3 Images Sometimes it could just lead to a poor experience - in other circumstances it could be much worse (Image: Johner Images via Getty Images)

UK consumers should be on high alert when making crucial financial or lifestyle decisions based on companies' online reviews, according to experts. TruthEngine, an independent platform specialising in detecting and preventing fake reviews, said its research, based on millions of datapoints and forensic analysis by a team of PhDs over a five-year period, indicated that just over 50% of online reviews were now fake.

‌



Put simply, that means consumers are frequently navigating a system where what appears to be authentic feedback is far from it. The organisation said the issue spanned every sector - from estate agents, financial advisers and travel companies to online retailers, energy firms and telecoms providers, no area of the market was immune.

‌



The problem, quite simply, was ubiquitous. Part of the challenge is behavioural, the researchers said. People who left reviews were far more inclined to do so following a negative experience than a positive one. That naturally distorted genuine feedback.

‌



View 3 Images People are being warned (Image: Chadchai Ra-ngubpai via Getty Images)

But compounding that was the increasing volume of manufactured reviews designed to artificially inflate ratings and sway purchasing decisions.

"Fake reviews have been an issue since online reviews became a thing just after the millennium and have been growing and getting more sophisticated ever since," said TruthEngine CEO Daniel Mohacek. "But now, with the arrival of AI, the problem has become so big that it is effectively institutionalised within modern ecommerce. Brands large and small are complicit and people are being lied to on an industrial scale."

‌



Paradoxically, the ratings shoppers place the most faith in may now be the most unreliable, as AI is increasingly behind them.

Firms displaying near-flawless scores, especially those around 4.9 out of 5, warrant scepticism. TruthEngine suggests these are frequently the most obvious indicators of tampering.

By comparison, businesses with ratings between two and three stars are now viewed as more trustworthy, reflecting a more genuine balance of favourable and critical responses.

‌



View 3 Images A study found that many reviews were fake (Image: Happy Kikky via Getty Images)

Daniel continued: "If it looks too good to be true, it usually is. When you see a brand with hundreds or even thousands of five-star reviews, that should be a massive red flag. It's now less a reason to trust them than, increasingly, not to trust them."

The problem has grown so severe that TruthEngine has introduced TruthMark, a verification badge given to companies whose reviews have been independently verified and thoroughly examined. While fake reviews may occasionally result in minor letdowns, such as a disappointing restaurant or lacklustre hotel, the dangers are considerably higher in other areas. Shoppers are progressively making choices about health products, financial services and children's items based on reviews that may be fabricated.

Article continues below

Daniel said: "It's one thing when a restaurant has duped you into buying a meal through fake reviews, but it's another thing altogether when you buy a product that could put your or your loved ones' lives at risk. And this is where things get very serious."

News.title: 'I feel so grateful to be alive,' says Kent University meningitis survivor
News.content:
Twenty-one-year-old Annabelle Mackay has spoken to the BBC about her experience of surviving a meningitis infection.

The University of Kent law student believes she may have contracted it on 5 March at Club Chemistry, or possibly on an evening before this when she was out at a different venue.

So far, there have been 29 cases of meningitis linked to the outbreak, with a vaccination programme being rolled out for students.

"I feel so grateful to be alive and to be here. I'm still dealing with the aftereffects now, but I think I just need to focus on my recovery and getting better and back to myself," Mackay told the BBC.

Read more here about why this outbreak has spread so quickly, and read more here about the symptoms of the condition.

News.content: In a statement to AFP news agency, the French armed forces said the reported incident did "not comply with the current instructions" and appropriate measures would be taken if the report was true.

News.title: Tenerife hit by snow as Storm Therese brings wind and rain to Canary Islands
News.content:
Authorities in Tenerife have activated emergency plans as Storm Therese brings wet, windy and wintry weather to the Canary Islands.

Outdoor events have been called off, roads are closed and some flights have been cancelled with weather warnings set to stay in force throughout the weekend.

Significant snow has already fallen over high ground in the centre of the island around the Mount Teide volcano.

Heavy rain will bring the threat of flooding while strong winds and rough seas will also pose a hazard.

Storm Therese was named by the Portuguese weather service earlier this week - largely because of the risk of disruption in Madeira and the Azores.

However the Canaries are now bearing the brunt.

News.title: Coroner 'not satisfied' boxing legend Ricky Hatton intended to take own life
News.content: Alison Much, sitting for the full inquest at Manchester South Coroner's Court in Stockport, concluded that while she was "satisfied that Hatton had carried out the acts which led to his death, [she] could not be sure he intended to take his own life".

News.title: Lily Allen's West End Girl portrait goes on show at National Portrait Gallery
News.content: "It seems to me the portrait reflects so many facets of the album - strength, power, vulnerability, determination and confusion, amongst many others - that it acts as a key to the whole listening experience. I love it."

News.title: Border security chief to step down after 18 months in the job
News.content: He will leave the job at the end of the month. A Home Office spokesperson said: "We would like to thank Martin Hewitt CBE QPM for his dedicated leadership since the creation of the Border Security Command.

Boxing.title: 'My relationship with Tyson is destroyed COMPLETELY': Emotional John Fury reveals breakdown with his son and wants NO part of his boxing return after Gypsy King's retirement U-turn
Boxing.content:
The road tightens until it feels like you are slipping off the map, hedgerows closing in before the landscape suddenly opens into a bare field. There is no grand entrance, no hint that one of boxing's most recognisable names lives here, just a fire burning against the cold, a pot simmering on a makeshift stove, and John Fury waiting.

Within minutes, before the tea has settled or the cameras have properly found their place, he delivers the line that defines everything that follows: his relationship with his son, Tyson Fury, is 'destroyed completely.'

He will not be at the fight against Arslanbek Makhmudov on April 11. He will not watch it. He believes Tyson is no longer the fighter he once was, 'gone' since the trilogy with Deontay Wilder, and he is adamant that those around him are to blame.

What unfolds over the next hour is not just an interview ahead of a heavyweight return, it is a father laying bare the collapse of one of boxing's most famous relationships, with all the bitterness, regret and lingering love that entails.

There is something starkly fitting about the setting. John Fury lives simply, almost defiantly so. The meal bubbling beside him - chicken, cabbage, broccoli, carrots, onion and a handful of other indistinguishable ingredients - is, he explains, what he eats three times a day.

No salt. No seasoning. Fuel, not pleasure. Occasionally he allows himself porridge with nuts, honey, ginger and garlic for breakfast. On weekends, ice cream 'for sugar.' Two pints of Guinness every Friday 'for iron.'

Tyson Fury's father John says his relationship with his son is 'destroyed completely'

The Gypsy King's father wants no part of his son's return to the boxing ring

Around him roam 30 horses, chickens scratching at the ground, sheep drifting across the field. It is a life stripped back to essentials, a world away from the multi-million-pound spectacle his son now inhabits.

Yet as he talks, it becomes clear that distance - physical and emotional - is exactly the point.

'My relationship with Tyson is destroyed', he tells Playbook Boxing. 'Boxing destroyed it completely. I'll say it on camera: I've never taken £10 off him in my life and I never will.

'I don't want Tyson's money and I don't need Tyson's money. Whatever he's got, good luck to him. But, don't forget who built his story when he was a kid. He didn't build it himself, did he? Me, his father.'

It is this sense of ownership, of having created something extraordinary, that makes the current estrangement cut so deeply.

And yet, for all the force in his words, there are moments when the facade falters. As the conversation drifts towards their relationship, his eyes redden, his voice tightens, and for a brief second he looks as though he might stop altogether.

'I was 30 seconds away from asking for a break there. I haven't really expressed these emotions before but they're strong and they're there,' he admits.

It is a striking contrast to the man often seen roaring at press conferences, flipping tables, offering people out and commanding attention with sheer volume.

John Fury pictured with Tyson Fury ahead of his press conference with Arslanbek Makhmudov

The Gypsy King will take on Makhmudov at the Tottenham Hotspur Stadium on April 11

Here, in the quiet of Mobberley, the emotion is not performative. It is contained, but only just.

What follows is a relentless, unfiltered assessment - not only of Tyson's current standing in the sport but of the decisions that, in John's view, have led him here.

'I think he's past his best. I'm a no-filter kind of guy - I say it how I see it. I love him, but there are too many people patting him on the back and telling him things that aren't true, building him up like he's invincible. He's not and he hasn't been for a while.

'Tyson has been gone since the Deontay Wilder fights, they finished him. Wilder completely done him. He's not got a leg underneath him. He's took a lot away from Tyson. Makhmudov is a problem for Tyson. I am the first one to say it.

'Listen, I understand now that Tyson is testing himself. But, I can tell you now, his legs aren't there anymore. I understand the only way he will believe that and see that is when the first bell rings.'

To John, the damage is both physical and irreversible. Three fights with one of the hardest punchers in heavyweight history, he argues, take something no training camp can restore.
